title: Cutting Family Ties
subtitle: Severing What Allah Has Commanded to Be Joined

verses.arabic: فَهَلْ عَسَيْتُمْ إِن تَوَلَّيْتُمْ أَن تُفْسِدُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَتُقَطِّعُوا أَرْحَامَكُمْ
verses.translation: So would you perhaps, if you turned away, cause corruption in the land and sever your [ties of] relationship?

verses.arabic: أُولَٰئِكَ الَّذِينَ لَعَنَهُمُ اللَّهُ فَأَصَمَّهُمْ وَأَعْمَىٰ أَبْصَارَهُمْ
verses.translation: Those are the ones whom Allah has cursed, so He deafened them and blinded their vision.

verses.arabic: وَالَّذِينَ يَنقُضُونَ عَهْدَ اللَّهِ مِن بَعْدِ مِيثَاقِهِ وَيَقْطَعُونَ مَا أَمَرَ اللَّهُ بِهِ أَن يُوصَلَ وَيُفْسِدُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ ۙ أُولَٰئِكَ لَهُمُ اللَّعْنَةُ وَلَهُمْ سُوءُ الدَّارِ
verses.translation: But those who break the covenant of Allah after contracting it and sever that which Allah has ordered to be joined and spread corruption on earth — for them is the curse, and they will have the worst home.

verses.arabic: وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ الَّذِي تَسَاءَلُونَ بِهِ وَالْأَرْحَامَ ۚ إِنَّ اللَّهَ كَانَ عَلَيْكُمْ رَقِيبًا
verses.translation: And fear Allah, through whom you ask one another, and the wombs [i.e., family ties]. Indeed Allah is ever, over you, an Observer.

brief_explanation: The Quran treats cutting family ties as an act that earns Allah's curse and spiritual blindness in this world — a severe consequence indicating how deeply this sin offends the divine order. Allah describes those who cut the ties He commanded to be joined as spreaders of corruption in the earth, linking family severance to societal destruction. The Quran pairs this sin with breaking covenants and spreading fasad, framing it not merely as a personal failing but as an attack on the fabric of human community. The Prophet (peace be upon him) warned that the one who cuts ties will not enter Paradise, and that Allah's mercy is withheld from gatherings where such a person is present.
capital_punishment: There is no hadd (fixed corporal punishment) for cutting family ties. It is classified as a major sin with divine punishment, primarily through the curse of Allah as described in the Quran, and through reckoning on the Day of Judgment. The Islamic state may impose ta'zir (discretionary measures) in cases where cutting ties involves material harm, deprivation of rights, or abandonment of dependents. Scholars emphasize that repentance and restoring ties — even unilaterally — is the prescribed remedy.
